title: Oversubscription Estimator Guide
summary: Understand access demand, aggregation demand, transport capacity, overhead reserve, and target peak utilization across network layers.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool compares downstream demand against upstream capacity at the access, aggregation, and core/WAN layers so oversubscription pressure can be identified before users feel congestion.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch network pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when designing switch uplinks, aggregation paths, WAN handoffs, or any shared network layer where many downstream ports or devices feed a smaller upstream link.
bullets: Early network design planning.
Expansion, migration, or upgrade review.
Existing-system troubleshooting or validation.
Explaining network tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible inputs on this tool and explain what each value represents in practical network planning terms.

items.label: Edge / downstream demand (Mbps)
items.description: The total traffic demand feeding into the access-layer uplink.
items.whyItMatters: This shows how much demand the access layer is trying to push upstream.
items.commonMistake: Using theoretical port sum when the goal is realistic peak traffic.

links.label: Build this from endpoint demand
links.note: Use Bandwidth Planner when endpoint count and per-device Mbps need to be modeled first.

items.label: Access uplink capacity (Mbps)
items.description: The usable upstream capacity leaving the access layer.
items.whyItMatters: This is the first shared boundary where downstream demand may exceed available capacity.
items.commonMistake: Assuming every downstream access port can run at full rate without checking the uplink.

items.label: Aggregation downstream demand (Mbps)
items.description: The total traffic demand entering the aggregation layer from downstream switches, closets, or segments.
items.whyItMatters: Aggregation demand can reveal bottlenecks that are hidden when each access switch is checked alone.
items.commonMistake: Adding access switches without summing their realistic peak contribution.

items.label: Aggregation upstream capacity (Mbps)
items.description: The usable uplink capacity leaving aggregation toward the core or transport boundary.
items.whyItMatters: This defines whether aggregation has enough upstream room for the load it collects.
items.commonMistake: Counting redundant links as if all of them are usable normal capacity.

items.label: Core / WAN expected peak demand (Mbps)
items.description: The expected peak traffic crossing the core, WAN, or transport boundary.
items.whyItMatters: This value connects local network design to the larger transport bottleneck.
items.commonMistake: Checking LAN switching without validating the smaller WAN or firewall path.

links.label: Project future demand
links.note: Use Growth Simulator when the transport boundary may become the next upgrade point.

items.label: WAN / core transport capacity (Mbps)
items.description: The usable bandwidth available at the core or WAN boundary.
items.whyItMatters: This is often the real limiter after local switching appears healthy.
items.commonMistake: Using LAN speed as the capacity value when traffic actually exits through a smaller WAN path.

links.label: Assess outage impact if this path fails
links.note: Use this when transport capacity also affects resiliency.

items.label: Overhead reserve (%)
items.description: A percentage allowance for protocol overhead, management traffic, retransmits, monitoring, and unmodeled variance.
examples: higher for tunnels or noisy links
items.whyItMatters: Overhead reserve prevents the estimate from treating all link capacity as clean payload capacity.
items.commonMistake: Leaving no reserve because the calculated demand barely fits.

items.label: Target peak utilization (%)
items.description: The maximum peak utilization you want a layer to reach before it is considered pressured.
items.whyItMatters: This turns raw capacity into an operating policy with real headroom.
items.commonMistake: Treating 95–100% utilization as acceptable for shared uplinks.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means the modeled layers stay inside the target utilization policy. Watch means one layer is close enough to review. Risk means a layer is likely oversubscribed under realistic peak demand.
bullets: Healthy: the entered assumptions leave practical operating margin.
Watch: the design may still work, but the assumption deserves review before relying on it.
Risk: reduce demand, increase capacity, change the topology, or validate the design more carefully before implementation.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most network planning mistakes come from checking one layer or one average value while ignoring peaks, overhead, path behavior, failure conditions, and growth.
bullets: Planning from theoretical line rate instead of usable sustained throughput.
Sizing for calm-period average traffic while ignoring peaks, bursts, retransmits, and maintenance activity.
Treating one network layer in isolation even though access, aggregation, WAN, VPN, and endpoint behavior interact.
Forgetting to document assumptions before the design becomes hardware, cabling, or service-provider work.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer documentation, firewall/router/switch limits, service-provider terms, cabling validation, monitoring data, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Real measurements from the installed network are better than estimates whenever they are available.
Protocol overhead, encapsulation, device behavior, cabling, switching, routing, and WAN conditions can change the result.
Healthy results still need validation against the actual switch, firewall, router, ISP, tunnel, or endpoint platform being used.
A design with documented headroom is usually easier to support, troubleshoot, and grow.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, vendor validation, production testing, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual hardware, firmware, service-provider limits, and network architecture being deployed.
Confirm stakeholder expectations for performance, outage behavior, security, and operational recovery.
Document assumptions before implementation and revisit them as traffic or topology changes.
